permuteDE 0.0.0.9000

permuteDE

Source: vignettes/permuteDE.Rmd
permuteDE.Rmd

Installation

permuteDE is designed to be run on Unix-based operating systems such as macOS and linux.

permuteDE installation currently requires remotes and BiocManager for installation of GitHub and Bioconductor packages. Run the following commands to install the various dependencies used by CHOIR:

First, install remotes (for installing GitHub packages) if it isn’t already installed:

if (!requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("remotes")

Then, install BiocManager (for installing bioconductor packages) if it isn’t already installed:

if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("BiocManager")

Then, install permuteDE:

remotes::install_github("corceslab/permuteDE", ref="main", repos = BiocManager::repositories(), upgrade = "never")

Introduction

This vignette provides a basic example of how to run permuteDE, a . permuteDE is applicable to and (see Advanced Options). Detailed parameter definitions are available under the Functions tab.

permuteDE is based on the premise that . The use of permutation testing allows permuteDE to .

permuteDE proceeds in two main steps. First, the user will . Then, .

You’ll need the following packages installed to run this tutorial:

library(Seurat)
library(permuteDE)

Example data

To demonstrate how to run permuteDE on a single-cell RNA-seq dataset, we’ll use a small dataset consisting of , originating from an experiment by et al. (), and available through the .

Pre-processing

permuteDE takes as input a Seurat, SingleCellExperiment, or matrix. see Advanced Options). Note that the exact pre-processing steps used are up to the user.

This tutorial uses a Seurat object; for details related to other object types, see Advanced Options.

Running permuteDE

permuteDE proceeds in two main steps:

  1. Function runDE() .
  2. Function permuteDE() .

Step-by-step:

Step 1: runDE()

. permuteDE is highly parallelized, so efficiency greatly improves as n_cores is increased. using default parameter settings.

The output contains .

Step 2: permuteDE()

. This permutation test yields a p-value that .

The output contains .

Plot

.

Advanced Options

Input object types

Seurat

For Seurat objects, the “RNA” assay is used if no input is provided for parameter use_assay. If no input is provided for parameter use_slot, the “counts” default slot (Seurat v4) or layer (Seurat v5) is used.

Some Seurat v5 objects have multiple layers (e.g., “counts.1”, “counts.2”, “counts.2”..) with different subsets of cells stored under the same assay. Currently, permuteDE requires a single layer containing all cells in an assay. For these cases, please re-organize the Seurat object prior to running permuteDE.

SingleCellExperiment

For SingleCellExperiment objects, only the use_assay parameter is needed. If not provided, it is set to “counts”. Please ensure that the relevant assay matrix includes both row and column names.

Matrix input

permuteDE parameters

Significance level & multiple comparison correction

The default significance level used by permuteDE is alpha = 0.05 with multiple comparison correction (parameter p_adjust_method) using FDR = 0.05. By default, permuteDE also requires that significant DEGs have a |LFC| > 0.5 (parameter lfc_threshold).

Filters

permuteDE uses various filters to reduce the number of DE comparisons:

  • Parameter <QQQ> indicates the minimum . It defaults to .
